title: After the Summit: Turkey Leverages NATO Spotlight Into Defense Push, but Europe's Doors Stay Half-Open
subhead: The Ankara NATO summit gave Turkey a global stage for its defense industry and strategic ambitions, but the gap between Erdogan's aspirations and European willingness to grant full access remains wide — while a Saudi-Turkish rail corridor through Syria and a framework law for Kurdish peace add new variables.

sections.heading: NATO Ankara Summit: Turkey Shows Off, Europe Stays Cautious
sections.body:
The July 7-8 NATO summit in Ankara was Turkey's moment to argue it belongs at the center of European defense — and Erdogan used it to maximum effect. The summit opened with a Defense Industry Forum where around 3,500 companies showcased Turkish defense capabilities, according to Kurdistan24. Erdogan told allies it is 'inconceivable to establish European security without Turkey' and pressed for Turkish inclusion in the EU's 150-billion-euro SAFE defense investment program.

NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte called the gathering 'a tremendous success,' and Trump — who had arrived berating allies and threatening to pull US troops from Europe — left saying there was 'tremendous unity,' RFE/RL reports. The summit produced concrete numbers: allies pledged 70 billion euros in military aid to Ukraine for 2026 with at least equivalent levels for 2027; NATO announced more than $50 billion in new procurements and $40 billion in counter-drone investments over five years; and Rutte highlighted that the 31 non-US allies had increased defense spending by $139 billion in one year, per Forbes.

But for Turkey specifically, the summit underscored a persistent tension. Ankara wants to move beyond being a supplier to becoming a strategic partner in joint production and technology cooperation, as SSB head Haluk Gorgun told Defence24. The Irish Times reported that Finland's President Alexander Stubb said Turkey needs to be 'as close to us as possible from a security perspective.' Yet France and Germany continue to block broader Turkish participation in EU defense programs. Under current SAFE rules, non-EU countries can supply no more than 35% of components in weapons financed through the initiative, and Turkey has not been offered the Security and Defence Partnership required to participate at all. Carnegie Europe's Sinan Ulgen noted that the 'political issues blocking Ankara's access to the SAFE program are the dispute between Turkey, Greece and Cyprus, but also France's ill will.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey used its host-nation advantage to make its case as indispensable, and the defense numbers are impressive. But the structural blockage — no EU security partnership, French and Cypriot vetoes — remains unresolved. The summit changed atmospherics, not architecture.

sections.heading: Turkey's Defense Industry: Booming, But Hitting a Ceiling
sections.body:
Turkey's defense sector has reached a scale that makes it hard for Europe to ignore. According to SIPRI, Turkey is the world's 11th-largest arms exporter, with a 1.8% share of the global market. Defense exports hit a record $7.1 billion in 2024, up nearly a third from the prior year, per Defense News. Turkish officials claim 2025 exports surged 48% and project $13 billion in 2026, according to the Irish Times and Kurdistan24.

The bilateral deal flow tells the story that SAFE doesn't. As Forbes analyst Gney Yldz documented, Turkey has built an industrial footprint in Europe through side doors: Baykar acquired Italy's Piaggio Aerospace and partnered with Leonardo on drone production (Italy gave conditional approval to the joint venture on June 17, per Reuters/AOL); Repkon is building a 155mm shell plant in Germany; Hurjet trainer jets beat Leonardo's bid for Spain's 30-jet contract; 20 Eurofighter Typhoons were sold to Turkey via a UK-led deal worth up to $10.7 billion, signed in October 2025 with a follow-on support deal in March 2026; and Turkey joined Germany's European Sky Shield air defense initiative. Poland and Romania already fly Turkish drones. None of this runs through SAFE. It runs around it.

Geopolitical Futures notes that Turkey's capabilities have moved well beyond drones 'into missiles, armored vehicles, naval platforms and defense electronics,' but warns that 'sustained growth will require Turkey to expand its entire defense industrial ecosystem,' including components, engines, and specialty materials that still depend partly on foreign suppliers.

The Forbes analysis is blunt: 'When the cheapest, fastest capacity is formally excluded from the sovereign fund but quietly contracted through national deals, you are not building autonomy. You are building a more complicated dependency and calling it independence. The Turkish parts will be in European kit either way. The only question SAFE actually decides is whether Europe pays a premium and waits longer to pretend otherwise.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense industry is outgrowing its political constraints — bilateral deals are proliferating even as the EU door stays shut. The question is whether industrial gravity eventually forces the institutional opening, or whether political friction keeps the gap wide.

sections.heading: Saudi-Turkish Rail Corridor Through Syria: A New Trade Route That Bypasses Israel
sections.body:
A proposed Saudi-Turkish railway linking the Persian Gulf to Europe through Jordan, Syria, and Turkey has emerged as a potential rival to the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) — and Israel is alarmed. Al Hurra reports that Israeli Transport Minister Miri Regev warned Prime Minister Netanyahu in a letter that the region is witnessing 'the rapid formation of regional partnerships in trade and energy that deliberately bypass Israel and pose a genuine strategic threat to national security.'

The corridor extends over 3,000 kilometers, carrying freight, passengers, oil, and natural gas. The route connects Saudi Arabia to Jordan's Port of Aqaba, then north through Amman, Damascus, and Aleppo to the Turkish border, linking to Turkey's rail network and European markets. Saudi officials frame it as a modern revival of the Ottoman-era Hejaz Railway.

Prior to the Saudi-Turkish memorandum of understanding, Turkey, Syria, and Jordan's transport ministries reached a separate agreement establishing a five-year technical roadmap to rehabilitate transport infrastructure across the three countries. CryptoBriefing adds that Saudi Arabia is 'actively exploring plans to reroute the IMEC corridor through Syria and Jordan, connecting to Turkey's Mediterranean ports, effectively bypassing Israel entirely.' The original IMEC northern corridor was designed to run through the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Israel's Haifa port — a plan now, as one source put it, 'collecting dust.'

A Foundation for Defense of Democracies report concludes that implementation is unlikely in the near term, citing 'significant financial and security challenges' and noting that 'no clear financing mechanism or governance framework has yet been established.' Israeli officials, meanwhile, are pushing Washington to intervene and restrain trade initiatives that exclude Israel.
sections.whyItMatters: The corridor is more signal than near-term reality, but the signal matters: post-Assad Syria is being incorporated into a Turkish-Saudi economic architecture that marginalizes Israel and gives Turkey a pivotal transit role between the Gulf and Europe.

sections.heading: Kurdish Peace Process: Framework Law Inches Forward, But Gaps Remain Wide
sections.body:
President Erdogan has instructed AKP lawmakers to pass a framework law for the Kurdish peace process before parliament goes on recess, according to BBC Trk and the pro-government Trkiye newspaper. But the details reveal how far apart the sides remain.

The government's draft, as reported by pro-government media, would be a one-time, temporary law conditioned on verification that the PKK has disarmed. According to senior AKP sources quoted by Trkiye newspaper, the National Intelligence Organization (MT) and Turkish Armed Forces (TSK) would prepare a verification report for the National Security Council, and implementation would begin only after the MGK confirms at least 80% compliance with disarmament and evacuation of militant positions. Parliament Speaker Numan Kurtulmu confirmed on June 30 that the law would be 'standalone and temporary,' applicable only within a specified timeframe.

The pro-Kurdish DEM Party, by contrast, demands a 'root law' — comprehensive legislation that would serve as the foundation for subsequent amendments to the Turkish Penal Code, the Anti-Terror Law, and execution legislation. The DEM Party also insists on incorporating the 7-to-8-item proposal conveyed by Abdullah calan through the mral Delegation. rmral Delegation member Pervin Buldan said on June 25 that 'even if calan does not accept the presented law, there is certainly a plan B and C. But this will not be a renewed armed struggle.' She described the framework law as consisting of 'approximately 7 or 8 articles' enabling members to 'benefit within a specific timeframe and come to Turkey or go to any other place they desire.'

Bianet reports that the DEM Party has warned that media leaks about the law's contents — including the conditional verification mechanism and the potential exclusion of calan — could harm the process, but emphasized that these are 'baseless' since no official draft has been shared.
sections.whyItMatters: The framework law is the critical next step in the post-PKK-dissolution peace process, but the gap between a conditional verification mechanism and a root constitutional reform is enormous. Whether this becomes a genuine opening or a temporary amnesty with limited scope depends on details that haven't been finalized.

sections.heading: Trump's NATO Visit: Disruptions and Grudging Praise
sections.body:
US President Donald Trump's visit to the Ankara summit was the most disruptive variable in an otherwise choreographed event. According to RFE/RL, Trump arrived having threatened to leave NATO over European reluctance to support US strikes on Iran, and publicly berated allies in his opening remarks — calling European nations disloyal for delaying US access to their bases, labeling the Iranian regime 'liars and scum,' and saying the Iran deal was 'over.' He also renewed threats to annex Greenland and alluded to potential trade sanctions on Spain.

Behind closed doors, NATO diplomats told RFE/RL the atmosphere was 'completely different' — Trump was 'in a good mood and much more diplomatic,' not repeating his public threats. He emerged calling the summit one of 'tremendous unity.' The final Ankara Summit Declaration endorsed the position that 'Iran must never have a nuclear weapon' and called on Iran to 'fully respect freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz.' Rutte pointed out that the US had made over 5,000 sorties from European air bases in recent months — a quiet rebuttal to Trump's claim that allies weren't helping.

For Turkey, Trump's presence was a mixed bag. On one hand, the summit's success and Trump's conciliatory exit gave Erdogan a diplomatic win. On the other, Trump's Iran position — and his threats against allies who didn't support it — put Turkey in an awkward spot given its relations with Tehran and Moscow. Turkish officials also hoped the summit might unlock US-made jet engines for the KAAN fifth-generation fighter program, Kurdistan24 reported, though no announcement on that front materialized.
sections.whyItMatters: Trump's volatility remains the biggest single variable in NATO's future. His public hostility and private accommodation pattern creates policy whiplash for allies, and Turkey — balancing relations with both Washington and Moscow — is particularly exposed to sudden shifts.

sections.heading: Water, Drought, and Leverage: Turkey's Tigris-Euphrates Dam Network as a Regional Tool
sections.body:
Floods in northern and eastern Syria in early June, followed by rising Euphrates levels after increased Turkish releases, have reignited debate over Turkey's use of water as a regional pressure tool. Asharq Al-Awsat reports that Iraq — which gets 60% of its water from Turkey — faced its worst drought in over 80 years in 2025, while Syria's Euphrates-irrigated farmland (over 640,000 hectares) remains dependent on Turkish releases.

Turkey's Southeastern Anatolia Project (GAP) built major dams including Atatrk, Keban, and Ilisu, sharply reducing downstream flows. The Ilisu Dam, which began operating in 2020, reduced Iraq's Tigris water share to nearly 60% of pre-dam levels. Turkey classifies the Tigris and Euphrates as transboundary rivers under its territorial sovereignty; Iraq and Syria want them treated as international waterways subject to fair-sharing rules.

A November 2025 Iraq-Turkey framework agreement on water and development marked a diplomatic step forward, though its full terms haven't been disclosed. Bilgay Duman, a Turkish water affairs researcher, called it 'an important development not only in Ankara-Baghdad relations, but also in redefining how shared resources are managed in the Middle East.' Turkey maintains that Iraq's water crisis stems from local mismanagement, not Turkish dams.
sections.whyItMatters: Water leverage is Turkey's quietest and most durable regional tool — it doesn't make headlines like drone exports, but it shapes the livelihoods and political stability of 40+ million people downstream. The 2025 framework agreement suggests some movement toward shared management, but the underlying asymmetry hasn't changed.

bottomLine: The Ankara NATO summit was Turkey's coming-out party as a defense-industrial power, and the numbers — $7.1 billion in 2024 exports, 3,500 companies at the Defense Industry Forum, bilateral deals spanning from Spain to Poland to Italy — are real. But the summit also revealed the limits of Erdogan's leverage: France and Cyprus still block Turkish access to the EU's 150-billion-euro SAFE program, Trump's volatility keeps all NATO allies on edge, and Turkey's domestic politics (opposition crackdowns, an incomplete Kurdish peace framework, water disputes) complicate the strategic partnership pitch. Meanwhile, the Saudi-Turkish rail corridor through Syria signals that Ankara is building economic architecture in its neighborhood that doesn't depend on European institutional approval — and that Israel and Iran hawks in Washington will be watching closely. Turkey is more indispensable to European security than ever; whether Europe is ready to pay the political price for full integration remains the open question.
sources: Forbes, RFE/RL, Kurdistan24, Irish Times, Asharq Al-Awsat, Al Hurra, Bianet, CryptoBriefing, Geopolitical Futures, Politico, Anadolu Agency, Defense News, SIPRI
